name: adaboost.m1
best_quote: At each iteration we apply a classifier to the weighted observations; misclassified observations have their weights increased, whereas correctly classified observations have their weights decreased. Finally a weighted vote is taken for the final prediction.

name: forward stagewise additive modeling
best_quote: Forward stagewise modeling approximates the solution by sequentially adding new basis functions to the expansion without adjusting the parameters and coefficients of those that have already been added.

name: exponential loss for classification
best_quote: The exponential loss function used by AdaBoost, L(y, f(x)) = exp(-y f(x)) where y ∈ {-1,1}.

name: binomial deviance
best_quote: Binomial deviance is more robust than exponential loss to misclassification and can be more resistant to outliers.

name: huber loss
best_quote: Huber loss provides a compromise between squared error loss and absolute loss that is more resistant to the occasional gross outlier.
formula: L(y, f(x)) = \begin{cases} [y - f(x)]^2 & |y - f(x)| \leq \delta \\ 2\delta|y - f(x)| - \delta^2 & |y - f(x)| > \delta \end{cases}

name: gradient boosted models
best_quote: Gradient boosting is a generalization of tree boosting that allows optimization of any differentiable loss function.

name: partial dependence plot
best_quote: One way to define the average or partial dependence of f(X) on XS is the marginal average of f.
formula: \bar{f}_S(X_S) = E_{X_C}[f(X_S, X_C)] = \int f(X_S, X_C) p_C(X_C) dX_C

name: steepest descent
best_quote: Steepest descent chooses hm = −ρmgm where ρm is a scalar and gm ∈ IRN is the gradient of L(f) evaluated at f = fm−1.

name: gradient boosting
best_quote: A possible resolution to this dilemma is to induce a tree T(x; Θm) at the mth iteration whose predictions tm are as close as possible to the negative gradient.

name: right-sized trees for boosting
best_quote: The simplest strategy for avoiding this problem is to restrict all trees to be the same size, Jm = J ∀m.

name: anova expansion
best_quote: One relevant property of η(X) is the degree to which the coordinate variables interact with one another. This is captured by its ANOVA (analysis of variance) expansion.

name: learning rate in boosting
best_quote: The parameter ν can be regarded as controlling the learning rate of the boosting procedure. Smaller values of ν (more shrinkage) result in larger training risk for the same number of iterations M.

name: subsampling in boosting
best_quote: Not only does the sampling reduce the computing time by the same fraction η, but in many cases it actually produces a more accurate model.

name: relative importance of predictor variables
best_quote: For a single decision tree T, Breiman et al. (1984) proposed [the sum over internal nodes of squared improvements] as a measure of relevance for each predictor variable Xℓ.

name: weak learner
best_quote: A weak learner is an algorithm for producing a two-class classifier with performance guaranteed (with high probability) to be significantly better than a coin-flip.

name: single index model in econometrics
best_quote: The M = 1 model, known as the single index model in econometrics, is an exception. It is slightly more general than the linear regression model, and offers a similar interpretation.

name: back-propagation equations
best_quote: Using this, the updates in (11.13) can be implemented with a two-pass algorithm. In the forward pass, the current weights are fixed and the predicted values f̂k(xi) are computed from formula (11.5). In the backward pass, the errors δki are computed, and then back-propagated via (11.15) to give the errors smi.

name: back-propagation
best_quote: This two-pass procedure is what is known as back-propagation. It has also been called the delta rule (Widrow and Hoff, 1960).

name: weight elimination penalty
best_quote: This has the effect of shrinking smaller weights more than (11.16) does.

name: convolutional networks
best_quote: This forces the extracted features in different parts of the image to be computed by the same linear functional, and consequently these networks are sometimes known as convolutional networks.

name: single hidden layer neural network
best_quote: Here we describe the most widely used 'vanilla' neural net, sometimes called the single hidden layer back-propagation network, or single layer perceptron.

name: weight decay
best_quote: A more explicit method for regularization is weight decay, which is analogous to ridge regression used for linear models (Section 3.4.1). We add a penalty to the error function R(θ) + λJ(θ).

name: universal approximator
best_quote: In fact, if M is taken arbitrarily large, for appropriate choice of gm the PPR model can approximate any continuous function in IRp arbitrarily well. Such a class of models is called a universal approximator.

name: pseudo residuals
best_quote: The components of the negative gradient computed at line 2(a) are referred to as generalized or pseudo residuals, r.

name: main effect
best_quote: The first sum in (10.40) is over functions of only a single predictor variable Xj. The particular functions ηj(Xj) are those that jointly best approximate η(X) under the loss criterion being used. Each such ηj(Xj) is called the 'main effect' of Xj.

name: decision stump
best_quote: Setting J = 2 (single split 'decision stump') produces boosted models with only main effects; no interactions are permitted.

name: early stopping
best_quote: A convenient way to estimate M* is to monitor prediction risk as a function of M on a validation sample. The value of M that minimizes this risk is taken to be an estimate of M*. This is analogous to the early stopping strategy often used with neural networks.

name: shrinkage in boosting
best_quote: The simplest implementation of shrinkage in the context of boosting is to scale the contribution of each tree by a factor 0 < ν < 1 when it is added to the current approximation.

name: partial dependence plots
best_quote: One way to define the average or partial dependence of f(X) on XS is the marginal average of f.

name: projection pursuit regression
best_quote: The projection pursuit regression (PPR) model has the form f(X) = Σm gm(ωᵀmX), where ωm are unit p-vectors of unknown parameters and the functions gm are unspecified and estimated along with the directions.

name: ridge function
best_quote: The function gm(ωᵀmX) is called a ridge function in IRp. It varies only in the direction defined by the vector ωm.

name: single index model
best_quote: The M = 1 model, known as the single index model in econometrics, is an exception. It is slightly more general than the linear regression model, and offers a similar interpretation.

name: gauss-newton search
best_quote: A Gauss–Newton search is convenient for this task. This is a quasi-Newton method, in which the part of the Hessian involving the second derivative of g is discarded.

name: single hidden layer perceptron
best_quote: Here we describe the most widely used 'vanilla' neural net, sometimes called the single hidden layer back-propagation network, or single layer perceptron.

name: sigmoid activation function
best_quote: The activation function σ(v) is usually chosen to be the sigmoid σ(v) = 1/(1 + e⁻ᵛ).

name: radial basis function network
best_quote: Sometimes Gaussian radial basis functions (Chapter 6) are used for the σ(v), producing what is known as a radial basis function network.

name: softmax activation function
best_quote: This is of course exactly the transformation used in the multilogit model (Section 4.4), and produces positive estimates that sum to one.

name: hidden units
best_quote: The units in the middle of the network, computing the derived features Zm, are called hidden units because the values Zm are not directly observed.

name: delta rule
best_quote: This two-pass procedure is what is known as back-propagation. It has also been called the delta rule (Widrow and Hoff, 1960).

name: batch learning
best_quote: The updates in (11.13) are a kind of batch learning, with the parameter updates being a sum over all of the training cases.

name: online learning
best_quote: Learning can also be carried out online—processing each observation one at a time, updating the gradient after each training case, and cycling through the training cases many times.

name: training epoch
best_quote: A training epoch refers to one sweep through the entire training set.

name: stochastic approximation
best_quote: This learning is a form of stochastic approximation (Robbins and Munro, 1951); results in this field ensure convergence if γr → 0, Σr γr = ∞, and Σr γ²r < ∞.

name: conjugate gradients
best_quote: Better approaches to fitting include conjugate gradients and variable metric methods. These avoid explicit computation of the second derivative matrix while still providing faster convergence.

name: variable metric methods
best_quote: Better approaches to fitting include conjugate gradients and variable metric methods. These avoid explicit computation of the second derivative matrix while still providing faster convergence.

name: hinton diagrams
best_quote: Figure 11.5 shows heat maps of the estimated weights from the training (grayscale versions of these are called Hinton diagrams.)

name: local connectivity
best_quote: Net-3 uses local connectivity: this means that each hidden unit is connected to only a small patch of units in the layer below.

name: weight sharing
best_quote: All units in a local feature map perform the same operation on different parts of the image, achieved by sharing the same weights.

name: feature maps
best_quote: The first hidden layer of Net-4 has two 8×8 arrays, and each unit takes input from a 3×3 patch just like in Net-3. However, each of the units in a single 8×8 feature map share the same set of nine weights.

name: receptive field
best_quote: In the first hidden layer (an 8×8 array), each unit takes inputs from a 3×3 patch of the input layer... their receptive fields overlap by one row or column.

name: hybrid monte carlo
best_quote: The particular MCMC approach that was used is called hybrid Monte Carlo, and may be important for the success of the method. It includes an auxiliary momentum vector and implements Hamiltonian dynamics in which the potential function is the target density.

name: automatic relevance determination
best_quote: In the latter method (ARD), the weights (coefficients) for the jth feature to each of the first hidden layer units all share a common prior variance σ²j, and prior mean zero. The posterior distributions for each variance σ²j are computed, and the features whose posterior variance concentrates on small values are discarded.

name: boosted neural networks
best_quote: Since boosting is typically most effective with 'weak' learners, we boosted a single hidden layer neural network with two or four units.

name: screened features
best_quote: Since the reduced feature sets come from the Bayesian neural network approach, only the methods that use the screened features are legitimate, self-contained procedures.

name: ard reduced features
best_quote: Since the reduced feature sets come from the Bayesian neural network approach

name: neural network computational complexity
best_quote: With N observations, p predictors, M hidden units and L training epochs, a neural network fit typically requires O(NpML) operations.

name: tangent distance
best_quote: This network was later outperformed by the tangent distance approach (Simard et al., 1993) described in Section 13.3.3, which explicitly incorporates natural affine invariances.

name: support vectors

name: FDA

name: penalized discriminant analysis
best_quote: The steps in FDA can then be viewed as a generalized form of LDA, which we call penalized discriminant analysis, or PDA:

name: kernel function

name: optimal separating hyperplane

name: bagged neural networks
best_quote: Bagging perturbs the data in an i.i.d fashion and then re-estimates the model to give a new set of model parameters. At the end, a simple average of the model predictions from different bagged samples is computed.

name: support vector classifier

name: regularization parameter
best_quote: It is easy to show (Exercise 12.1) that the solution to (12.25), with λ = 1/C, is the same as that for (12.8).

name: linearly separable
best_quote: Optimal separating hyperplanes are introduced in Chapter 4 for the case when two classes are linearly separable.

name: nonseparable case
best_quote: Here we cover extensions to the nonseparable case, where the classes overlap.

name: svm path algorithm
best_quote: Here we describe a path algorithm (in the spirit of Section 3.8) for efficiently fitting the entire sequence of SVM models obtained by varying C.

name: multiclass svm
best_quote: The support vector machine can be extended to multiclass problems, essentially by solving many two-class problems. A classifier is built for each pair of classes, and the final classifier is the one that dominates the most.

name: one-versus-one classification
best_quote: A classifier is built for each pair of classes, and the final classifier is the one that dominates the most

name: overfit boundary
best_quote: A large value of C will discourage any positive ξi, and lead to an overfit wiggly boundary in the original feature space

name: smooth boundary
best_quote: a small value of C will encourage a small value of ∥β∥, which in turn causes f(x) and hence the boundary to be smoother.

name: tuning parameter
best_quote: The tuning parameter of this procedure is the cost parameter C.

name: epsilon-insensitive loss
best_quote: This is an "ε-insensitive" error measure, ignoring errors of size less than ε. There is a rough analogy with the support vector classification setup, where points on the correct side of the decision boundary and far away from it, are ignored in the optimization.

name: svm for regression
